caps.reason: You cannot be placed abroad without a passport, so your readiness is held at needs prep until you have one in hand. It is the first thing to sort, and SA passport turnaround is usually a few weeks.

reason.Full SA Code 8/EB: A standard car licence covers most farm roles but limits machinery and driving work.
reason.Code 10/14 (LDV/HDV): A Code 10/14 licence widens USA H-2A and Canada LMIA options that need vehicle operators.
reason.Heavy machinery / Code EC: A heavy machinery or Code EC licence is a strong asset for mechanised and equipment-operator roles.
reason.Tractor experience only (no licence): Tractor experience helps but a formal licence broadens your eligible roles.
reason.None: No driving licence narrows your options to general labouring roles only.

reason.Excellent — daily heavy labour now: Excellent fitness meets the physical demands of seasonal farm work.
reason.Good — fit and active: Good fitness is sufficient for most farm roles abroad.
reason.Fair — would need to build up: Build up your fitness before departure, as farm days are long and physical.
reason.Poor — health concerns: Health concerns are a significant blocker for physically demanding farm work; seek advice first.

reason.Livestock / dairy: Livestock and dairy handling is in high demand for NZ and Canada year-round roles.
reason.Machinery / equipment operator: Machinery and equipment operation is a sought-after skill that unlocks better-paid roles.
reason.Crops / grain: Crop and grain experience is in strong demand across UK and Canada programmes.
reason.Horticulture / orchard: Horticulture and orchard work is the core of the UK Seasonal Worker Visa intake.
reason.Irrigation: Irrigation experience is valued on larger commercial farms abroad.
reason.Poultry: Poultry experience suits the UK winter poultry window and similar roles.
reason.Packing / grading: Packing and grading is useful but more entry-level, with modest hiring priority.
reason.Other: Confirm how your farm experience maps to overseas roles; demand varies by sector.
reason.None yet: No farm experience recorded yet; UK horticulture entry roles accept beginners, so this is workable.

rules.empty_reason: No farm experience recorded. Livestock, dairy, crop and machinery skills get hiring priority, so add the work you have done.

ranges.reason: No farm experience yet; UK Seasonal Worker Visa entry roles still accept beginners.
1 to 3 years of farm experience meets the bar for most seasonal programmes.
3 to 6 years of experience unlocks the NZ Global Workforce Seasonal Visa and skilled roles.
6 or more years of farm experience makes you a strong applicant for skilled and supervisory roles.

reason.UK (Seasonal Worker Visa): Prior UK Seasonal Worker Visa work is a strong track record for repeat placements.
reason.USA (H-2A Visa): Prior USA H-2A experience significantly strengthens future applications.
reason.Canada: Prior Canadian farm work helps with LMIA-backed roles and possible return.
reason.New Zealand: Prior New Zealand farm work supports seasonal and skilled re-entry.
reason.Other country: Prior overseas farm work abroad improves your applications.
reason.None: No overseas farm work yet; workable, though returners are favoured for repeat seasons.

rules.empty_reason: No prior overseas farm work recorded; this is fine for first-time applicants to the UK scheme.

reason.Valid — 18+ months remaining: Passport valid with good runway, meets typical seasonal visa requirements.
reason.Valid — under 18 months remaining: Passport valid but renew soon, as some visas need more runway.
reason.No passport: No passport, apply immediately; this is the first blocker.

reason.Current (within 6 months): Current police clearance, meets visa-ready bar.
reason.Older than 6 months: Police clearance older than 6 months, most visa applications require a fresh one.
reason.Applied — waiting: Police clearance applied for; SAPS turnaround is typically 4 to 8 weeks.
reason.None: No police clearance, apply now; SAPS turnaround is typically 4 to 8 weeks.

reason.Under R10k: Below the typical UK upfront budget of R20 to 30k for visa fee and flight.
reason.R10k – R30k: Covers the UK upfront budget; USA H-2A reimburses most costs anyway.
reason.R30k – R60k: Comfortable budget for visa fees, flights and first weeks abroad.
reason.R60k+: Strong financial position for any seasonal route.
reason.Prefer not to say: Budget not declared, share privately with us if it shapes your options.

reason.None: No prior visa refusals, which keeps your applications clean.
reason.Yes — UK: A prior UK refusal must be declared on future applications; understand and address the original ground before reapplying.
reason.Yes — USA: A prior USA refusal must be declared; H-2A and other US visas weigh prior refusals, so resolve the original reason first.
reason.Yes — other country: A prior visa refusal must be declared on future applications; address the original reason before reapplying.
reason.Multiple: Multiple prior refusals are a serious risk factor that visa officers weigh heavily, so get specialist advice before reapplying.

reason.Native / first language: English as first language easily clears the low language bar for farm roles.
reason.Fluent: Fluent English is more than enough for farm work abroad.
reason.Conversational: Conversational English is sufficient for most farm roles, which have a low language bar.
reason.Basic: Basic English is workable for many farm roles, though it limits supervisory positions.

reason.true: Bringing a spouse or children adds visa steps and cost; relevant mainly for longer-term Canada routes.
reason.false: No dependants to bring, the simplest profile for seasonal farm relocation.

reason.2026: A 2026 departure aligns with the upcoming recruitment cycles; start applications now.
reason.2027: A 2027 horizon gives ample time for documents and visa processing.
reason.2028: A 2028 horizon is well ahead; use the time to build experience and savings.
reason.Just exploring: Exploring only, no urgency means you can plan strategically.
